title: False Positive Tracking
dataSources: IDS alerts + analyst disposition data (true/false positive)
spl:
index=ids sourcetype="snort:alert"
| join max=1 signature [| inputlookup signature_dispositions.csv]
| stats count(eval(disposition="false_positive")) as fp, count as total by signature
| eval fp_rate=round(fp/total*100,1)
| where fp_rate > 50
| sort -fp_rate
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.IDS_Attacks
  by IDS_Attacks.signature span=1d
| sort -count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Intrusion_Detection / IDS_Attacks acceleration for the raw IDS feed volume side; analyst dispositions in `signature_dispositions.csv` are still required for the full false-positive rate logic.
description: High false positive rates waste analyst time and cause alert fatigue. Systematic tracking drives tuning improvements.
value: High false positive rates waste analyst time and cause alert fatigue. Systematic tracking drives tuning improvements.
grandmaExplanation: We track which network-threat rule names keep getting marked as false alarms so the team can tune the rules that waste the most time.
implementation: Track analyst dispositions for IDS alerts (true positive, false positive, benign). Calculate false positive rate per signature. Flag signatures with >50% FP rate for tuning. Report on overall alert quality metrics.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: IDS TA + analyst workflow.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: IDS alerts + analyst disposition data (true/false positive).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Track analyst dispositions for IDS alerts (true positive, false positive, benign). Calculate false positive rate per signature. Flag signatures with >50% FP rate for tuning. Report on overall alert quality metrics.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ids sourcetype="snort:alert"
| join max=1 signature [| inputlookup signature_dispositions.csv]
| stats count(eval(disposition="false_positive")) as fp, count as total by signature
| eval fp_rate=round(fp/total*100,1)
| where fp_rate > 50
| sort -fp_rate
```

Understanding this SPL

**False Positive Tracking** — High false positive rates waste analyst time and cause alert fatigue. Systematic tracking drives tuning improvements.

Documented **Data sources**: IDS alerts + analyst disposition data (true/false positive). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): IDS TA + analyst workflow. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ids; **sourcetype**: snort:alert. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ids, sourcetype="snort:alert". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by signature** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **fp_rate** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where fp_rate > 50` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare results with raw Snort or Suricata logs on the sensor, with Cisco Firepower Management Center, or with the IDS or IPS management console event view for the same time range. Confirm that severity and counts line up, then verify field extractions, tags, and index permissions in Splunk.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (FP rate by signature), Line chart (FP rate trend), Table (signatures needing tuning).
visualization: Bar chart (FP rate by signature), Line chart (FP rate trend), Table (signatures needing tuning).
knownFalsePositives: Signatures covering common tools, SSL or malware inspection, and authorized security software can look like false alarms in bulk. Analyst habit (marking everything benign during an incident) and CSV backfill errors also skew false-positive rate—keep disposition discipline and validate the lookup.
